name: Muslim pilgrims during Hajj
shortDescription: Muslim pilgrims undertaking the Hajj pilgrimage are not deities in themselves, but their collective presence and actions during this sacred journey hold immense religious significance within Islam. They embody devotion, unity, and submission to God (Allah) as they perform the rituals prescribed in the Quran and the Sunnah (Prophet Muhammad's teachings and practices).
description: While not deities in the traditional sense, Muslim pilgrims during Hajj represent a powerful collective force and a visual manifestation of faith. Their journey to Mecca, the holiest city in Islam, and their performance of the Hajj rituals, are considered a profound act of worship and a fulfillment of one of the five pillars of Islam. The pilgrims, drawn from diverse backgrounds and nationalities, symbolize the universal brotherhood and sisterhood within the Muslim community (Ummah). Their unified actions, such as circling the Kaaba, praying together at the plains of Arafat, and stoning the pillars representing Satan, are potent symbols of devotion, repentance, and the rejection of evil. The Hajj experience is believed to cleanse pilgrims of their sins and bring them closer to God. Their collective prayers and supplications during this time are considered particularly efficacious. The Hajj pilgrimage is a transformative experience for the individual pilgrim, but it also reinforces the collective identity and spiritual unity of the global Muslim community. The rituals performed are rooted in the traditions of Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham) and are seen as a reenactment of his devotion to God. The pilgrims' journey is therefore not merely a physical one, but also a spiritual one, connecting them to the prophets and the origins of their faith.
epithets: Duyuf ar-Rahman (Guests of the Most Merciful)

sources.title: Hajj: The Islamic Pilgrimage to Mecca and the Holy Places
sources.author: F.E. Peters
